questions.explanation: Basic rule: drop -en. 'starten' → ik start. No extra letters added or removed.
Double-consonant rule: drop -en AND drop one of the doubled letters. 'zit-ten' → ik zit. The doubled 't' was only there to keep the 'i' short in the infinitive.
Long-vowel rule: drop -en AND double the vowel to keep the sound long. 'lo-pen' has open syllable → ik loop closes the syllable, so 'oo' is needed to keep the long sound.
Z → S at the end of a stem. 'reizen' → ik reis. Dutch never ends a word with voiced 'z' — it devoices to 's'. (NOT 'ik rees' — the vowel doesn't change.)
V → F at the end of a stem. 'schrijven' → ik schrijf. Same principle as Z → S: voiced consonants devoice when there's no vowel after them.

questions.q: Why does 'leren' become 'ik leer' (with double e)?
options: Because all stems double their vowels
Because 'le-ren' is an open syllable (long e), and 'leer' is closed — needs 'ee' to stay long
Because 'r' always doubles the preceding vowel
questions.explanation: In 'le-ren' the syllable is open (ends on a vowel sound) so 'e' is long. When you close the syllable to make 'leer', you must double to 'ee' to preserve the long sound.
Stem + t. The stem of 'lezen' is 'lees' (Z→S), so 'jij' adds -t: jij leest. Same for hij/zij/het.

options: When 'jij' comes AFTER the verb, drop the -t: 'Werk jij vandaag?'
questions.explanation: Inversion rule: when 'jij' (or 'je') follows the verb in a question or after a fronted element, the -t is dropped. 'Jij werkt' but 'Werk jij?'
